class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Telemedicine and Digital Health
learning_objectives: Define telemedicine, telehealth, and digital health and their distinctions
Trace the evolution of telemedicine from early telephonic consultations to modern platforms
Understand the regulatory landscape governing telehealth practice
readings: Telemedicine and e-Health: Principles and Practice Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The COVID-19 pandemic transformed telemedicine from a niche service to a mainstream care delivery model. This course examines the technology, policy, and clinical practice of digital health.
key_concepts: Telemedicine: remote clinical services using technology
Telehealth: broader term including education, administration, remote monitoring
Digital health: encompasses telemedicine, mHealth, wearables, AI, genomics
Modalities: synchronous (live video), asynchronous (store-and-forward), RPM
State licensure, prescribing, and reimbursement regulations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live demonstration of a telehealth platform showing provider and patient interfaces, virtual waiting room, and documentation workflow
setup_requirements: Telehealth platform access (Zoom for Healthcare or Doxy.me)

review.value: Real-time video/audio consultation between patient and provider, replicating an in-person visit
Asynchronous transmission of clinical data (images, records) for later review by a specialist, common in dermatology and radiology

learning_objectives: Describe the technical components of a telehealth system
Evaluate video conferencing platforms for clinical use
Understand bandwidth, latency, and connectivity requirements for clinical telehealth

lecture_notes.introduction: Telehealth effectiveness depends on reliable technology. Understanding the technical stack from network infrastructure to peripheral devices is essential for successful implementation.
key_concepts: Video conferencing: codec, resolution, frame rate, encryption requirements
Network requirements: bandwidth (minimum 1.5 Mbps), latency (<150ms), jitter
HIPAA-compliant platform requirements: BAA, encryption, access controls
Peripheral devices: digital stethoscopes, otoscopes, dermatoscopes, vital sign monitors
Digital divide: broadband access disparities and health equity implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Technical assessment of a telehealth setup: bandwidth test, peripheral device connectivity, and HIPAA compliance checklist review

review.value: End-to-end encryption, Business Associate Agreement with platform vendor, access controls, and audit logging of sessions
Disparities in internet access and digital literacy that prevent equitable telehealth utilization, disproportionately affecting rural and low-income populations

learning_objectives: Explain remote patient monitoring program design and implementation
Evaluate consumer and clinical-grade wearable devices
Understand data integration challenges from RPM devices into EHRs
readings: CMS Remote Patient Monitoring Billing Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Remote patient monitoring extends care beyond the clinic walls, enabling continuous data collection and early intervention for chronic disease patients.
key_concepts: RPM program components: devices, data transmission, monitoring platform, clinical workflows
Common RPM conditions: heart failure, COPD, diabetes, hypertension
Device types: blood pressure cuffs, pulse oximeters, glucometers, weight scales
Consumer wearables: Apple Watch, Fitbit, CGMs - clinical validity and limitations
Data integration: device-to-cloud-to-EHR pipeline, FHIR Observation resources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up an RPM workflow: pair a Bluetooth blood pressure cuff, transmit a reading to a cloud platform, and view it in a monitoring dashboard

review.value: CMS CPT codes 99453 (setup), 99454 (device supply/data), 99457/99458 (monitoring time) enabling billing for RPM services
Clinical-grade devices are FDA-cleared for diagnostic use; consumer devices may inform but not diagnose, with varying accuracy

learning_objectives: Evaluate mobile health applications for clinical and patient use
Understand FDA regulation of mobile medical applications
Design mHealth interventions using behavior change frameworks
readings: FDA Guidance: Policy for Device Software Functions

lecture_notes.introduction: There are over 350,000 health-related mobile apps. Separating evidence-based tools from wellness toys requires understanding regulation, evidence, and design principles.
key_concepts: mHealth categories: disease management, medication adherence, wellness, clinical tools
FDA regulation: when a mobile app qualifies as a medical device (SaMD)
Digital therapeutics (DTx): prescription-grade software interventions (e.g., reSET for substance use)
Evidence evaluation frameworks for mHealth (ORCHA, MARS scale)
Behavior change theory applied to mHealth design (COM-B model, nudge theory)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate three diabetes management apps using the MARS (Mobile App Rating Scale) framework, comparing features, evidence, and usability
setup_requirements: Smartphones with sample health apps installed

review.value: Evidence-based software programs that deliver medical interventions to patients, often FDA-authorized and sometimes prescribed like drugs
Software as Medical Device: FDA classifies software intended for medical purposes based on risk level (I, II, III) with corresponding regulatory requirements

learning_objectives: Design a telehealth program for a specific clinical use case
Develop workflows integrating telehealth into existing clinical operations
Create evaluation frameworks for telehealth program outcomes

lecture_notes.introduction: Successful telehealth requires more than technology. It demands thoughtful program design, workflow integration, change management, and continuous evaluation.
key_concepts: Needs assessment: identifying the right use cases for telehealth
Workflow design: scheduling, check-in, visit conduct, documentation, follow-up
Provider training: webside manner, virtual physical exam techniques
Patient onboarding: technology setup, expectations, troubleshooting
Outcome evaluation: access, quality, satisfaction, cost-effectiveness, equity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a teledermatology program: map the patient journey from referral through store-and-forward image capture to specialist review and follow-up

review.value: The virtual equivalent of bedside manner: eye contact with camera, appropriate lighting, minimizing distractions, verbal confirmation of understanding
Combining in-person and virtual visits based on clinical need, patient preference, and visit type appropriateness

class_schedule.topic: Future of Digital Health: AI, Genomics, and Virtual Reality
learning_objectives: Evaluate emerging digital health technologies and their potential impact
Understand the convergence of AI, genomics, and digital health
Assess ethical and equity implications of advanced digital health tools
readings: Topol E. Deep Medicine: How AI Can Make Healthcare Human Again (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: The next wave of digital health combines artificial intelligence, genomic data, extended reality, and ambient computing to fundamentally transform how care is delivered and experienced.
key_concepts: AI-powered telehealth: virtual triage, ambient clinical documentation, automated screening
Precision medicine meets digital health: genomic-guided telemedicine
Virtual reality in healthcare: surgical training, pain management, rehabilitation
Ambient intelligence: voice assistants, smart rooms, passive monitoring
Equity imperative: ensuring innovation benefits all populations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Experience VR-based pain management demonstration and review AI-powered symptom checker accuracy studies

review.value: AI systems that passively listen to patient-provider conversations and automatically generate clinical documentation, reducing documentation burden
FDA-authorized VR programs for chronic pain, anxiety, and rehabilitation showing clinically significant outcomes in randomized trials

questions.question: Compare synchronous, asynchronous (store-and-forward), and remote patient monitoring telehealth modalities. For each, provide one clinical specialty where that modality is most effective and explain why.
questions.answer: Synchronous (live video): most effective in primary care and mental health because these specialties rely on real-time patient-provider conversation, observation of patient demeanor, and interactive assessment. Asynchronous (store-and-forward): most effective in dermatology because skin conditions can be photographed, clinical history attached, and reviewed by a specialist without real-time interaction, enabling efficient high-volume consultations. RPM: most effective for chronic heart failure management because daily weight and vital sign monitoring enables early detection of fluid retention and decompensation, allowing intervention before hospitalization.

rubric.key: Appropriate specialty match for each modality
Clear clinical rationale for each match

questions.question: A rural health clinic wants to implement RPM for 200 hypertensive patients. Describe the key technical and clinical components they need and identify two major barriers they may face.
questions.answer: Technical components: Bluetooth-enabled blood pressure cuffs, patient smartphones or cellular-connected devices, cloud platform for data aggregation, EHR integration for documentation, alerting rules for abnormal readings. Clinical components: enrollment criteria and protocols, clinical monitoring workflows (who reviews data, how often, escalation thresholds), patient education and onboarding process, provider response protocols for alerts. Major barriers: (1) Broadband connectivity - rural areas often lack reliable internet, requiring cellular-connected devices rather than WiFi/Bluetooth to smartphone approaches. (2) Patient digital literacy - elderly hypertensive patients may struggle with device setup and daily measurement routines, requiring simplified devices and hands-on onboarding support.

rubric.key: Complete technical and clinical component list

questions.question: Explain the FDA's regulatory approach to Software as a Medical Device (SaMD). When does a mobile health app require FDA clearance, and when is it exempt?
questions.answer: The FDA regulates software intended to be used for medical purposes (diagnosis, treatment, prevention of disease) as SaMD. An app requires FDA clearance when it transforms or analyzes medical data to provide diagnosis or treatment recommendations (e.g., an AI app that interprets ECG rhythms). Apps are exempt when they are low-risk wellness tools (fitness trackers, calorie counters), EHR access portals, or tools that simply display data without interpretation. The FDA uses a risk-based framework where higher-risk functions (e.g., radiation dose calculation) require more rigorous regulatory pathways (510(k), De Novo, PMA) while lower-risk functions may qualify for enforcement discretion.

rubric.key: Correct description of FDA SaMD approach
Accurate examples of when clearance is required

questions.question: Design the patient journey for an asynchronous teledermatology program, from primary care referral to specialist follow-up. Include at least 6 steps and identify the technology needed at each step.
questions.answer: Patient journey: (1) PCP identifies skin concern and places teledermatology referral in EHR (technology: EHR referral module). (2) Patient receives notification to capture images via patient app (technology: HIPAA-compliant smartphone app with dermoscopy attachment). (3) Patient photographs lesion following guided protocol (3 images: overview, close-up, dermoscopic) and answers clinical questionnaire (technology: structured image capture app with clinical history form). (4) Images and history transmitted securely to dermatology review queue (technology: store-and-forward platform with HIPAA encryption). (5) Dermatologist reviews images, history, and any prior records within 48 hours, renders assessment (technology: clinical review workstation with calibrated monitor, EHR access). (6) Dermatologist documents findings and recommendations in shared EHR, triaging to in-person visit if needed (technology: EHR documentation, secure messaging to PCP). (7) Patient notified of results and follow-up plan through patient portal (technology: patient portal with secure messaging).

rubric.key: Complete patient journey with at least 6 steps
Appropriate technology identified at each step

major_assignments.title: Digital Health Program Design and Business Case
major_assignments.description: Design a comprehensive digital health program for a healthcare organization. Include clinical use case selection, technology assessment, workflow design, implementation plan, financial analysis, and evaluation framework.
objectives: Apply digital health concepts to design a real-world program
Conduct a technology assessment and vendor evaluation
Develop a financial model demonstrating program sustainability
Create an evaluation framework measuring clinical and operational outcomes
deliverables: Technology requirements and vendor comparison matrix

criteria: Evidence-based program design with clear clinical rationale
Thorough technology assessment and vendor evaluation

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of telehealth concepts, technology, and regulatory knowledge

class_participation.description: Engagement in discussions, technology demonstrations, and case analyses

major_assignments.description: Digital health program design and business case

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of telehealth modalities, technology, and regulation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including program design, mHealth, and emerging technologies

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Active participation in telehealth demonstrations is part of the grade. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day up to 5 days. Program design milestones must be met for iterative feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Technology evaluations may be conducted in pairs. All written deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Vendor marketing materials must be critically evaluated, not simply restated.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with webcam and microphone, smartphone, reliable internet connection. Telehealth platform access will be provided.
